speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to your sister after you got married. Did she live by you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived together. And she was spending-- she lived in New York, and she came out every weekend to Toms River, to New Jersey. And we were together. But she got sick in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She got mentally sick, and she had a nervous breakdown-- here, and we tried to help her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went through a lot of sadness with her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She had very bad-- spare us always. They called it 'sisophenia'.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- that sometimes she was 100% all right, but sometime no. Just it came like that. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She died here, in California. But she didn't die from that. She died. She got a heart attack, and she died. I think it's 40 years now. I'm not sure. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --we came in-- in '60 here to California. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She died 1990. I don't know. Sharon, when did Auntie Hannah die?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back now on your life and your experience, what do you think about it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I think about it? It was meant for me to live and go through so many time hell and come back from there. That's what I think about it. I'm like a cat-- always on the bottom and have to lift myself up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am a Capricorn. That's what they say-- that you have to lift yourself up. I don't know. But I went plenty time through hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back last year with your daughters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't go to Auschwitz. That I said I was here once, and it was enough for me. They went. Both of them went. And they took pictures. And they showed me the pictures. What I said-- I never saw Auschwitz like that with grass and trees. When I was there, it wasn't grass and trees. And I went back to my house-- what they let me in. And I remember everything. I find the kitchen with the white tiles the way I remembered, and the bathrooms with the white tiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Claire, when you think about your great great grandchildren, who you might never have a chance to meet, what is that you would want to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would like to tell them, no matter what they're going through in life, I hope that never a generation would have to go through what we went. That happened once in a lifetime that you had to be killed because you are a Jew. I hope that never happens to no generation. But ups and down happen to everybody, so don't give up. Try to fight when you are down, and believe in something-- that it will change. If you ever hear my story, never give up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] Because nobody in the world had always good in their life. [CRYING] Just they didn't go through that horror what that generation went through-- to kill them out, to burn them because they were Jews. I hope that never happen in the world again to no generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] One of your daughters is here. Would you like to introduce her to us? And you also have some pictures you'd like to show us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I wanna introduce my daughter, Sharon. She's my oldest daughter. She was born in New Jersey. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was the most beautiful child. I was so happy having you. And since then, you are my wonderful daughter taking good care of me all the time. And I love you very much, sweetheart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I love you too, Mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm Sharon. And I was born in Lakewood, New Jersey, because Toms River didn't have a hospital [LAUGHS] in 1950. I was born on August 28th. My sister, Robin, was born December 14, 1952. She's married and lives in Houston. Her husband's name is Bennett Greenspan, and they have two beautiful children-- Elliott Daniel, who was born December 11, 1985, and Brianna Leigha was born October 19, 1987. And they're growing up way too fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And you have some family pictures that you want to show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to see my parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we're gonna bring the pictures in, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures are my mothers and my father, but I don't remember when it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their name is Czanka Brand and Benjamin Schoenfeld. Do you remember about how old they were at the time of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very young. She got married, I think, when she was 17 year old. And that wasn't their married picture here. I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: World War I in 1919, when the war started-- he was in that picture a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: This one where you are on the left at the top, this family picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left? I am on the top? I don't see it. Oh. My mother, and my brother, and my sister, Hannah, and my sister; my father is here with-- The mustache.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mustache. But I don't see myself there. Just I see Serene and my-- and Hannah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're all the way on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you're all the way on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now I see it. Now I see it. Thank you. Because my left eye is very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a special occasion that grandparents-- because that uncle came back from America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken where you're with your family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. But I don't know, sweetheart, because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That big family picture was taken when my uncle from America came to visit us-- that we should remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I will point out who is on it. Where should I start to point out?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on the bottom in the middle there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am on the bottom in the middle. And after me is-- where is Alice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't that Alice next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice from right, and then my cousin, Andy. He's the youngest one. And for my left is another cousin, but he died in-- in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then where's Uncle Al in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Al is with my sister, Sarah, on the top. That's my sister, Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah. And Aunt Hannah is on the left side--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I can see her. You see from here. But I can see the rest of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is the Uncle Al who brought you to the United States--

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought me here to this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who's the man in the middle with the hat that's seated with the mustache?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandfather, my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is your wedding picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, August 18, in Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, September the 18th. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did you get married, Mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn, New York. In New York, and it was taken after the war. It was probably in 1900 when I came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK. Clara, what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me with my tattooed hand before they cut it out and they sewed in. Because I didn't wanna have it in New York. I didn't wanna be reminded all my life about my past. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember the last name? This is the house where my mother and her family lived in Munkacs. This picture was taken in May of 1993, when we went back there. My sister and I took my mother back there for the first time since the war. And she recognized the windows of the house. And we were invited in to see the house by the people who lived there now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture, Claire. Who's--

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was our family picture. And as I look Brianna now, I think it was three years ago taken. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's your granddaughter on your lap?

speaker: interviewer
text: And behind him is your son-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind him, my son-in-law, Bernard Greenspan. And next to him is his wife Robin, my daughter, Robin Greenspan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to her is Sharon Weisz, my daughter. And I'm between them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was about three-- three years ago, 1991?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?
